comments.title: Gratitude
comments.content:
I would like to say a few words of deep gratitude to all the ecosophians who participated in the gift giving to JMG last Magic Monday. Not only did it make for one thunderbolt of a gift for our favourite archdruid. But as some of you started stating your intentions in that thread, you also gave me personally a tremendous gift.

Occult themes have been tabo in our relationship ever since the start. Well; in the days after the last Magic Monday, leading to my departure for Great Britain. We had an apparition late in the evening as we were going to sleep. My partner noticed it first and commented on it, a sudden pleasant smell, incense. I reassured her and she went to sleep. I took some precautions and then sensed my way around. I got an intuition it came on behalf of someone.
Next morning we discussed it and I explained her her first “ghost” as she later described it. And days later I went to Glastonbury.
As I came back I discussed my trip with my partner, and I was prepared to omit a lot of it, but somehow she never showed any discomfort. So I asked her why she was taking it so remarkably well and she answered, that after that “pleasant ghost” she has no problem with it, it is good. And it has been a part of our daily conversations ever since. In her words: “I can be for you what Steffen is for Regine”

So words of thanks; you have healed a great rift in our relationship; there are none sufficient.
Thank you.

John you have the best commentariat on the internet.
(And I am profoundly grateful for the mercy of you not seeing my face right about now)

Thank you,
V

replies.title: Re: Gratitude
replies.content: Good heavens. I'm delighted to hear this -- and yes, I really do have the best commentariat on the internet.

replies.title: Re: Gratitude
replies.content:
Excellent news. And may the child who is coming be blessed by parents who are on the same team! :)

PS. It was a lovely and well considered gift, and may it bring much joy and weal to the Archdruid we all love.

replies.title: Re: Gratitude
replies.content:
Oh what a pleasant result. And the fairies returned your luggage too! Congrats.

Jack

comments.title: Psychic vampirism recovery/healing
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

I am writing to ask you how to recover from an episode of psychic vampirism. My story is a bit odd to say the least, and I'm not sure if you'll believe me, but this period in my life I went through years ago has made what seems to be an indelible mark on my health from which I've never fully recovered.

It all started when I met a family friend who was very much a fundamentalist Christian, or apparently so. He had been a minister and teacher before in different churches and schools in the fairly distant past by that time. At some point, he introduced me to the "baptism in the Holy Spirit" along with speaking in tongues and such. A lot of things started happening after that.

My anxiety levels went up, my sleep went to hell, and I had a couple of extremely unusual dreams, unlike any I've had before or since. In both dreams, there was no set of events that actually happened, except for the characters staring at me. The first dream had a vampire staring at me with large unblinking eyes. The second dream had a small creature with wrinkled grey skin and cold blue eyes staring at me and jittering a bit. Both of these nightmares were unnerving at the least, but at the time I had become a Christian and chalked both episodes up to the devil.

Later on, the man confessed to being a hypnotist. I didn't put two and two together at the time because I was still a minor and really didn't know that much. He mentioned hypnotizing some students as a playful diversion when he was a public school teacher, putting them in trance, and making them lie rigid between two desks and so on. I remember saying you had to know you were being hypnotized to be hypnotized, and he said, "No you don't."

Much later on, after I had gotten away from his influence, I read a book on the history of hypnosis written by some French author, I forget who off the top of my head. The author listed some of the signs of trance induction, like twitching eyelids or tingling sensations in the body, or a feeling of heat in the eyes, and so on. And all of a sudden I was struck: the signs he listed were exactly the same as the signs I was told were signs of "God's anointing" and things I could feel while I was under the power of God. This apparent baptism in the holy spirit was actually the creation of a trance state masquerading as a religious experience. In other words, he masked the hypnotic education as a form of religious education. It was the same process, just under a different name.

Another odd thing happened too while we were talking on the phone one night, incidentally about the devil and the kinds of things he did. I heard to my left a cold high laugh out of nowhere, and it scared me to pieces. It lasted for maybe 3 seconds and I fled the room for the evening. I had rebuked the voice whatever it was, and the man laughed through the phone when he heard me do that.

I haven't had any auditory or other hallucinations since that moment, so I don't think I'm schizophrenic. I think it was really an episode of having come into contact with a bad character. Several years after I got away from him, I also figured out he had dropped a bunch of suggestions like "Mary had a little lamb, the lamb became a sheep, the sheep became a Charismatic and died from lack of sleep." And so on. It became clear to me that in our hours and hours of conversation on the phone, he had slipped in all kinds of suggestions to try to fuck me up.

I think the two dreams I had were really my subconscious mind trying to warn me about that guy now, but I had no idea of that possibility when I was a kid.

Do you have any advice for understanding my experience better than I have, or what I might do to improve my health and wellbeing? It's been quite a while since it happened, but I still feel not so great on a regular basis. The man in question died a few years ago of a heart attack, so he is long gone in the physical sense at least. I have tried some magic and meditation, as well as self-hypnosis. I'm helped a bit by the first two, but the last experiment didn't do much.

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Psychic vampirism recovery/healing
replies.content:
First, you've described a kind of spiritual malpractice that's embarrassingly common these days. I encourage you to find and read a copy of Dion Fortune's book Magical Self-Defence, which covers such things in quite a bit of detail.

Second, I don't recommend self-hypnosis at all for someone who's been through the kind of experience you've undergone. You need to strengthen the boundaries between conscious and subconscious minds, not erode them further!

Third, I recommend a daily banishing ritual of some kind, such as the Sphere of Protection or the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram, as essential magical hygiene for everyone these days, and especially those who have had encounters with nasty magic, as you have.

replies.title: Re: Psychic vampirism recovery/healing
replies.content: I don’t have JMG’s experience with these things, but may I suggest the use of an affirmation? That technique seems particularly suited for repeating whenever one of his creepy little rhymes runs through your mind, and for repeating when you are having trouble falling asleep. I have found affirmations very helpful for dealing with intrusive thoughts while trying to fall asleep and for countering attempts to undermine my autonomy and self-concept.

comments.title: Polarity and the Etheric Body
comments.content: It has been mentioned in previous Magic Mondays that those with a significant number of intimate partners might get their etheric body into a state where it is full of holes or otherwise weakened because it has been mingled with and subsequently pulled away from many other etheric bodies. Will having a significant number of different polarity ritual partners using workings similar to those in your book on polarity magic pose a similar risk to the etheric body given those rituals also involve a shared current of etheric energy? If not, what is the difference?

replies.title: Re: Polarity and the Etheric Body
replies.content: There are significant differences between modes of etheric interaction that involve physical contact and those that don't. The etheric body itself only extends an inch or so beyond your material body, and so it has to be touching another body to mingle with that body to any degree. Doing etheric polarity with with six feet of space and an altar in between the participants, by contrast, doesn't mingle the bodies as such -- it simply creates a flow of etheric energy, of the sort that goes on naturally (for example) any time you stand in sunlight or bathe in cool or cold water. Thus it's much easier on the system, even if many different partners are involved.

replies.title: Re: Polarity and the Etheric Body
replies.content:
How does one go about recovering from unwanted sex? I'm talking forcible rape, child molesting, or coercion ("I'll feed your starving kids, poor refugee mother, if you'll have sex with me").

I hate to think of the karma perpetrators have, but how do victims heal before Judgment Day?

replies.title: Re: Polarity and the Etheric Body
replies.content:
The psychological and spiritual dimensions of that are outside my range of competences; you'd want to talk to a good psychotherapist with experience in that very challenging field. In strictly etheric terms, it's essential to give the etheric body time and the necessary resources to heal. The old custom of purification through ritual bathing in cool or cold water -- never cold enough to shock the system! -- has much to recommend it, since water cooler than body temperature carries away unwanted etheric traces and helps the etheric body restore itself. (Even if you haven't had so hideous an experience, showering or bathing daily in cool or cold water is recommended by many trad occultists; I do it myself, for example.)

Sunlight, fresh air, and nourishing food are also important to provide the etheric body with the materials it needs to heal. Meditation, relaxation, anything else that decreases stress fosters etheric healing as well. If the person can tolerate another person's touch without tension or fear -- I know some survivors of such things can't -- professional massage is very beneficial, since it helps open up the etheric channels in the body, and that helps the etheric body clear away wastes and recover.

None of this is enough by itself, of course; there also has to be the psychological and spiritual dimension -- but again, that's outside my skill set.

comments.content:
Hi JMG and happy birthday!

I'm glad you had a good time in England. I have a practical herbalism query for you (as I make my tinctures for the CGD Bardic initiation). Two I did yesterday (Sunday) went fine (though the chamomile ash didn't get that grey) but due to a mishap I didn't have enough lemon balm and my local Melissa stockist was out when I called in earlier today, so I'll have to order it. The macerated Melissa residue is currently with its filter paper in a sealed plastic bag inside a sealed glass jar in a cupboard.

1. Can I still use the residue?

2. If so, am I storing it correctly?

3. And is there a time limit beyond which I should macerate another batch of Melissa?

In a TSW note, divinations I did prior said I'd need to "redo the herbs" but the ones on the day would be fine. I had no idea what that meant until it did!

With gratitude from Sydney for your generous time and wisdom.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I would recommend calcining the residue as soon as possible; it can be stored indefinitely once it's been reduced to ash, but before then it can mold or otherwise sour, which spoils it. Then macerate more in a separate batch, and combine the calcined ashes from the two batches.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Great, thank you! I'll get on with it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Second JMG's note that the ash can be kept indefinitely. Secondly, it can be daunting to get to a high degree of calcination using heat alone. Another thing that can be done is this: when you feel the ash has gotten as light as you can get it, pour it into a glass container with some *distilled* water and let it sit for awhile in a warm place, giving it a stir or a shake every now and then. Then, after a few days, filter the water into a decent sized ceramic, flat bottomed dish (I used a casserole for this) and put it in a warm oven and gently evaporate the water. Warm, not hot. You will find that the vegetable salt has crystalized on the bottom of the casserole. Keep back the ash you have filtered out. It can be worked with more if you choose to do so.

comments.title: Harassment by a Stalker
comments.content:
Some months ago I moved to a new town in a neighboring state. For associated reasons the past two or three years have been extraordinarily difficult and over-the-top stressful, and have concluded with a personal tragedy.

But it seems the powers have decided that I haven’t got troubles enough. Over the past six months it has developed that I have a stalker who nastily harasses me whenever I go into the center of this small town and he happens to be there.

It plays out like this. I am walking down the street past all the cute little shops - I am fortunate to live in quite a charming town - and suddenly, without warning, from a couple of feet behind me a voice screams:

KILL YOURSELF PERVERT!

This has happened several times. The latest encounter was a couple of evenings ago; he didn’t scream, but he followed me a short way down the block, saying nasty things. Basically he’s stalking me.

It’s a homeless guy. The police tell me he has a history of doing this to older white men for no discernible reason.

But I suspect he’s not only mentally ill - this seems incontrovertible - but also may have a touch of psychism. For indeed I am gay. I think he picks up on that, and the gist of his verbal harassment suggests that he is motivated by a mentality akin to that of the Fred Phelps school of religion (“all f*gs must die”).

The police have told me his name, and I’ll soon be filing a restraining order. But will that be enough? This character is a real nutter and I don’t feel safe with him around.

Another factor may be that I have been practicing a good deal of magic lately. The Middle Pillar and the Rose Cross are my daily practice, and I often invoke a (benefic) planet on appropriate days as well. I don’t know but I suspect that to a deranged person with some degree of psychism this may constitute an irresistible provocation.

I really don’t want to saddle myself with a lot of magical chores, but I feel it might be a good idea to protect myself in any way I can. I’m open to constructive suggestions.

Also, at the suggestion of the police I’ll probably soon be getting some pepper spray to carry on me.

replies.title: Re: Harassment by a Stalker
replies.content:
First of all, please accept my condolences for the personal tragedy as well as the rest of it. What a horrible thing to have to go through.

In terms of practical steps, if you're used to the Middle Pillar exercise, one good option is to pick up a copy of Israel Regardie's short book The Art of True Healing if you don't already have it -- it's also included in his anthology Foundations of Practical Magic -- and begin using the special working he adds to the Middle Pillar for practical purposes, as described there. Invoke Saturn/Binah with the intention of binding the guy so he cannot harass you any further. Do this every day as part of your regular practice. I've had very remarkable results from such workings, and that (plus pepper spray and a restraining order) will likely be quite adequate.

replies.title: Re: Harassment by a Stalker
replies.content: Thanks! I will do this.

replies.title: Re: Harassment by a Stalker
replies.content:
Dear JMG,

For your questioner here I feel moved to mention that for some profoundly helpful advice on dealing with unwanted attention on a nonmagical level there is Gavin de Becker's book, THE GIFT OF FEAR. The main lesson I took from it was, as he says, "engage, enrage." If you're familiar with his book, I'd be very curious to know your take on it.

Cetiosaurus

replies.title: Re: Harassment by a Stalker
replies.content: I'm not familiar with it at all. Sorry.

comments.title: possible ill outcomes of prayer
comments.content:
Hello all,

Thanks JMG for your ever generous sharing of knowledge and wisdom on MMs.

Since my partner died last month, my parents have been praying the Rosary every night for both of us. I don't know how it is going with him but for me life could be smoother. I keep encountering mundane obstacles which seem worse than one might expect. What I need to sort out legally and in regards to his remains is not going smoothly. My house is too cold - the fire no longer lights - even the coffee machine has stopped working. I am worn out and despondent from having to try so hard with everything yet making no progress.

Although I no longer go to church I didn't think the prayers could hurt. Now I wonder whether they are inadvertently bringing me ill fortune.
Not consciously antagonistic to the Christian pantheon and in fact I pray to some of them myself.

What do you think?

replies.title: Re: possible ill outcomes of prayer
replies.content: Is it possible that your parents are praying for you to return to their faith? In that context, prayer can sometimes function as evil magic -- a kind of spiritual bullying.

replies.title: Re: possible ill outcomes of prayer
replies.content:
While they may not be, it would be a certainty that my mother's sister would be doing that.
Thanks.

replies.title: Re: possible ill outcomes of prayer
replies.content: Another possible reason could be linked to the end of the relationship. Something similar happened at the end of my marriage, everything started breaking, one thing after the other. I'm not sure entirely what was going on, but each time I mustered my will and solved the problem I felt better. I also had a bit of help from others which makes a difference.

replies.title: Re: possible ill outcomes of prayer
replies.content:
What an intriguing notion. The day he died my phone went haywire too.

If I may ask, you and your spouse broke up, not that they died?

Help from others is two-edged for me as some of them are also in a church and I do not want to be stealth evangelized.

replies.title: Re: possible ill outcomes of prayer
replies.content: Yes it was just a separation but it was a jarring one where our life was very intwined with a family etc and he simply disappeared. I'm not sure why everything started breaking with it but it was a strange mix of difficult to deal with and helping me out of a rut trying to fix stuff.

It's entirely possible to do classic GD-style magic and occultism without going gaga, but it's usually necessary to avoid the initiation rituals -- not that many people, in particular, seem to be able to go through the Adeptus Minor ritual without suffering from spectacular ego inflation. It's worth noting that Dion Fortune replaced the GD rituals with a different set of initiations, and her tradition hasn't had anything like the same problems with swollen-headed egomaniacs as the classic GD and its offshoots.

The classic GD mostly works by way of ritual magic, which can be a faster path but is also usually a riskier one. The others work mostly by way of meditation, which can be slower but is more reliable.

comments.title: Stonehenge vs Avenbury
comments.content:
Question to all: has anyone visited both Stonehenge and Avebury and noticed any difference between the two sites?

Some quotes below but I remember JMG saying that in Avebury there was a long ago change in which the barrow was sealed up and worship changed from that of the dead or maybe better to say the source of power moved away from the dead and to the sun with the introduction of the stones.

In Dion Fortune's terrific introduction to Glastonbury she compares Stonehenge with Avebury.

She didn't like Stonehenge: "The soul of the place reeks of primitive man, his blood-sacrifices and his dark fears. On every hand lie the barrows of his burying and the tumuli of his sacrifices" and "The grey stone scan never forget - the blood has sunk too deep into them".


"Very different is Avebury, the great sun-temple of its glory. Here an invisible sun, formed by the magic of the priests, shines ever into the hearts of men. Here is healing and joy, and a wisdom which is not of age".


My recent experience was of visiting Stonehenge prior to Glastonbury and then Avebury. Unfortunately, it is very difficult for me to "feel" anything of a place though at Stonehenge, we had a pleasant walk back from the stone circle to the visitor's center across the fields with mounds nearby.

I found my irritations growing while in Glastonbury and then onwards to other areas. Usual irritations when modern life intrudes such as news or in heavy tourist zones. On our way back east we stopped in Avebury. I wish we could have spent more time there but did make most of a circuit around the henge. I did feel relieved and more positive after this quick visit and I would love to return and spend longer on site one day.

replies.title: Re: Stonehenge vs Avenbury
replies.content:
I've been to both sites several times, and the energies of the two sites are very different. Stonehenge is brooding and withdrawn -- there's a tradition in some British occult circles that it was deliberately wrecked at the end of its period of use to shut down its energies, which had turned destructive due to certain rites worked there. Avebury is more open, and also more balanced -- it once had two complete stone circles within a third, like this:
[IMAGE:0]
Keep in mind that they were built at two very different times. Avebury was begun relatively early in the history of henge monuments, and it echoes the spirit of an expansive and optimistic time, when the old earth-centered cult of the long barrows (by then grown decadent) had been overthrown and replaced by a worship of the gods and goddesses of the sky. Stonehenge is very late in the same age -- the last and most spectacular product of the age of henges -- and it rose while the culture of Neolithic Britain was hurtling toward the disasters that overwhelmed it in turn.

replies.title: Re: Warts
replies.content:
Note that warts are viral, caused by viruses from the same family that produced the horn-like growths on the probable original animals behind the jackalope legend:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shope_papilloma_virus

The conventional medical treatment for warts is weak acids for breaking up keratinous masses:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keratolytic

As I understand it, a papillomavirus blindly structures the epidermis that it has infested so that it will break up into keratin particles full of infectious virus when exposed to water. Presumably, this is due to having been repeatedly "rewarded" in the past for doing that with propagation to new hosts, because water is a better medium than dry ground for dispersing viruses to potentially reach vulnerable cracked, scratched, blistered, or thin skin with exposed cell surface receptors. But normally, a papillomavirus blindly plays the long game, setting up a wart that only sheds a little bit of virus-laden particles at any given time. In that connection, maybe what the acid does is "trick" the blind structural mechanism by which the papillomavirus has set up the epidermis to only conditionally break up into particles, like that effect where you can trick most of the parasitic horsehair worms inside a mantis to emerge to mate by touching the hind end of the mantis to a small amount of water. Alternatively, maybe it is the dampness alone that is sufficient to break up the wart, and the acid is just there to kill the released virus, or perhaps protect against other infections that might normally result from leaving skin damp for an extended period.

replies.title: Re: Warts
replies.content: Fascinating!!! Thanks for the info - yes perhaps it’s the acids in it that break it down. It did work quite well either way.

replies.title: Re: Warts
replies.content:
What has worked reliably for me and others I know is Celandine (Chelidonium majus). I broke a leaf from a vigorous looking plant and painted the warts with the beautiful bright orange sap oozing from the stem. I repeated this for several days until the warts were gone. I like to explain my need to the plant spirit and thank it for its help.

This is a quirky personal anecdote, and does not constitute medical advice. What you do with your warts is your business.

Good luck.

replies.title: Re: Warts
replies.content:
I've had some warts on my nose and between my eyebrows for a while now, but over the past month or two, they've started going away, some amazingly fast and with seemingly no residual signs after months of seeming stuck. Here's what I've done/has changed, but since I've taken a bit of a "throw everything at the wall and see what sticks" approach, I can't pinpoint what has been most helpful:

1. Apply Apple Cider Vinegar with a q-tip to the wart, preferably multiple times a day. When I've been more consistent with this, I've seen noticeable physical degradation of the wart (getting soft, top layers peeling/flaking away).

2. Healing Hands directed to them (you should be quite adept at this. For what it's worth, I've gotten a sense that it's more important to focus on what is "causing" the warts, deep down, than on the wart "itself.")

3. Washing the affected area more thoroughly and regularly (I had previously avoided washing my face with soap to not dry it out too much, causing a reaction of too much oil, leading to pimples. I started using Dr. Bronner's Baby Mild soap, and that seems to be a happy medium for me).

4. Changes in stress and self-image. These warts appeared in one of the most stressful and saddening times of my life, and as I've processed that and tried to deal with it (including with the physical stuff mentioned above, like working out, spending a moderate amount of time in the sun, eating better, and so forth), they've gone started going away, even when otherwise stubborn to the approaches above. comments.title: Ethics
comments.content:
Hello JMG! Welcome back and Happy Birthday to you. Thank you for update and report on what looks to have been a marvelous trip to merry ole England!

My question: Are there any records of ethics in occultism from Hellenistic or Renaissance times? We do have Manly P Hall’s 10 Basic Rules, and DF’s Practical Occultism etc, and the general “Harm none”. But just wondering if the ancients had a specific code of ethics regarding occultism that may have survived?

Thank you!
Jill C Yogaandthetarot

replies.title: Re: Ethics
replies.content:
The Picatrix, my go-to source for early medieval magic, states as a matter of course that before you take up the study of magic you should study all of philosophy, and the three branches of philosophy in those days were logic, physics, and ethics. The same principle guided the old Neoplatonist theurgists in their magical work. Thus there doesn't seem to have been a specific code of magical ethics at that time; the idea seems to have been that of course you'll have put in plenty of time studying, say, Aristotle's Nicomachean and Eudemian Ethics, Epictetus, and so on, and developed your own ethical understanding from those.

One of the great differences that set the ancient view of ethics apart from ours is that they weren't much into handing down rigid rules. Instead, they sought to develop a degree of understanding from which it was obvious that being selfish, cruel, etc. was just plain stupid.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Do you think the various Godlike characters from 40k are eligible for a sympathetic and more occultly realistic rework, like what you did for the Lovecraftian ones?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's a good question I can't answer. There are at least two issues here. The first, on an esoteric level, is whether the WH40k deities are well enough grounded in the collective unconscious for such a reworking to be anything more than a parody. Cthulhu, even though he was invented in passing by one of history's most neurotic writers to fill a slot in a single short story, tapped into the deep places of the collective unconscious so effectively that -- even though he only appeared in that one story of Lovecraft's -- he very quickly became an iconic figure in weird-tales fandom and then in pop culture generally. Millions of people who've never read a single Lovecraft story have some idea of who and what Cthulhu is. I'm not sure if that's yet true of Tzeentch, Cegorach, or any of the other WH40K deities.

The second, on the practical level, is copyright. I was able to pen my tentacle novels and publish them, first, because Lovecraft himself released his deities into the public domain, and encouraged other writers to use them, and second, because he and his fellow authors of the weird tales scene between the wars have been dead (yet dreaming?) long enough that their copyrights have expired. I don't happen to know the copyright status of Nurgle, Slaanesh, et al., but you'd have to look into that.

The one figure from WH40K who's unquestionably made the jump to the deep places of the collective unconscious already is of course the God-Emperor of Mankind. He's even got his own mortal instantiation these days:
[IMAGE:0]
But you'll have to get your own sense of whether the others are ready, and legally available, for this sort of thing.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
My exposure to the lore from the earlier Warhammer game is pretty indirect, but I'd gotten the impression that the Ruinous Powers in particular were presented as being in contrast to the other powers in the setting as having pervasively harmful addictive and warping effects on mortals that, implicitly, other powers would not have had even if they were to gain superficially similar purviews and cultuses. That'd be something a prospective reworker would want to look into, since another potentially important aspect of realism would be the implied message that some entities are just not within range of any given mortals' comparative advantage to help de-maleficize, however rightful such a project might in any given case otherwise be.

(Not that it'd be literally impossible, like, you could probably de-maleficize 40k Slaanesh if you could allocate comparably many quintillions of psychically-attained-space-elf-years to the practice of austerities or Epicurean-style intensively reflectively-disciplined enjoyments, for the sake of cleaning up after their forebears. But who has that kind of space-elf-time just lying around unused, and also how would you keep them protected in the interim?)

(...and now I have the image in my head of a small circle of Eldar engaging their full powers in a desperate struggle to keep from falling to Chaos while still performing an ordinary tea ceremony all the way through to the end)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I read the section in your recent Polarity Magic book about church services and energy exchange with keen interest. I was particularly interested in the section on clergy who cannot handle the energy loads and destroy their lives, as I know a very traditional priest in the Anglo-Catholic sphere who is currently battling entrenched alcoholism. I can attest he is a notably talented handler of energy—when he gave me a blessing once a few years ago, my entire head felt like it was sparking energy (in a positive way). Yet he still went off the rails. But I will save my questions on that situation for another day.

My current question is about the diagram showing how astral and etheric energy is exchanged from congregants to celebrants and vice versa in “an effective religious service” in Chapter 8.

I am now an Eastern Orthodox Christian, and I attend a church with a dome. I distinctly sense an energy current ascending from the crown of my head up to the dome during services. It’s unmistakable and much stronger than the currents to/from the priests, which I really have to focus and hone in on to feel out. The one heading up from my crown to the dome is so present that it is impossible to ignore. It feels like flames or a beam of light continuously streaming up. It’s really, really intense.

It’s not in your diagram, and I’m wondering how you would interpret it? Is there somewhere I can read more about it? Notably, I haven’t felt it in dome-less EO churches, but I also haven’t spent as much time in those.

I would appreciate any insight you have.

My other question is about the currents to/from the priest as depicted in the diagram, which I have to make such an effort to latch into. Since trying to consciously tap into them on Sundays, I’m exhausted on Mondays. This never happened before. I’m wondering if I’m doing something wrong.

Grateful for any advice you have. (Also happy to hear from other Orthodox or liturgical Christians!)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The diagram in the book only deals with the specifically polarity-based side of inner work in group ritual. There's more going on! In a Christian church that still uses old (and therefore effective) rituals, the sense of flames rising from the crown of the head ought to be standard -- it's the Pentecost experience, in effect, and it's not a matter of polarity. (It has much more in common with kundalini, though in a controlled and nuanced way.) A very, very large book could be written on all the energetic dimensions of the old Christian rituals, and their interface with traditional Christian architecture.

2) Yes, you're doing it wrong. Don't try to tap in consciously -- if you do that you'll put too much of your own vitality into it. Relax -- let it happen, don't make it happen.

comments.title: cultural appropriation for the win

Meanwhile, if you haven't seen it already, there's an interesting short Japanese novel, Eclipse by Keiichirō Hirano. It's set in the late 15th century, and deals with a young Dominican monk who is en route from Paris to Florence to acquire recent translations from the Corpus Hermeticum. On the way, he comes to a village near which dwells an alchemist ... complete with alchemical symbolism and a geometrical enigma or two.

comments.title: Mullen

I have a lovely bunch of mullen that has volunteered on the south side of my home. This spot has been used for various wildflower plantings and a few herbs over the decades, and as far as I know, unlike the feral sweet peas, hollyhocks and poppies, mullen has never been planted there. I assume the seeds stuck to the dog's coat or perhaps were brought by wild birds a couple years ago.

What magical properties belong to mullen and what reasons would you all ascribe to it popping up here? It's about two or three weeks from bloom, it looks like.

BoysMom

PS I have a little sagebrush growing out of my rock retaining wall between the upstairs and downstairs driveway facing doors, and I understand that sagebrush discourages unfriendly visitors, so I'm quite pleased to see it make itself at home there.

replies.title: Re: Mullen
replies.content: Mullein, Verbascum thapsus, is a protective plant ruled by Saturn; it chases off hostile spirits, and a plant growing near the house is especially good as it keeps the house and all in it safe from noxious energies. I'd say the local nature spirits like you and decided to give you a gift. Let it complete its life cycle undisturbed; you'll probably end up with a nice little mullein bed, which will radiate protective blessings to a considerable distance.
